name: HMS P 39 (P 39)
events: The history of HMS P 39 as compiled on this page is extracted from P 39's patrol reports and logbooks and was created in October 2014. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in May 2026.
15 Nov 1941 P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) departed her builders yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HNoMS Molde. (1)
16 Nov 1941 AT 1450 A/16, P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) arrived at Holy Loch from Barrow. She was no begin a period of trials and training (work-up period).
On arrival at Holy Loch she was taken over from the shipyard having successfully completed her acceptance trials.
[As there are no logs available for HMS P 39 no further info is available on the work-up period.] (1)

16 Dec 1941 Around 1600 A/16, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) departed Holy Loch for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Bay of Biscay to intercept German battlecruisers Scharnhorst and Gneisenau in case they would leave Brest.

Passage south through the Irish Sea was made together with HrMs O 21 (Lt.Cdr. J.F. van Dulm, RNN) and FFS Junon (Cdr. J. Querville). They were escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR) which parted company in position 270° - Wolf Rock - 3 miles at 1825 A/18.
Upon completion of the patrol P 39 was to proceed to Gibraltar.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed. Also no patrol report has been located.] (1)

20 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2144 A/20, the Flag Officer Submarines (F.O.S.) informed HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) that her patrol was cancelled and she was to proceed to Gibraltar through positions '09' and thence through position 45°00'N, 11°54'W. Further routing would be signalled by the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.). (2)

21 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1410 A/21 the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic's (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) regarding her onward route to Gibraltar upon leaving patrol; onward route through the following positions; 'X', 43°00'N, 11°54'W, 'Y', 36°00'N, 11°00'W, 'Z', 350° - Spartel - 5 miles. (2)

22 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1436 A/22 the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following information / orders to HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); HMS Westcott (Cdr. I.H. Bockett-Pugh, DSO, RN) approximate position 42°00'N, 14°00'W at 0800 A/22 is routed through position 185° - Cape St. Vincent - 15 miles, speed 15 knots. You are not to pass through position 39°20'N, 11°20'W before 0200 A/23. HMS Westcott has been told to keep 30 miles clear of you. (2)

24 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1606 A/24, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) informed HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) of the following; HMS Vanoc (A/Cdr. C.F.H. Churchill, RN) off Cape Ortegal at 1228 A/24th has been ordered to Gibraltar by shortest route at best speed. She has been given your movements. (2)
26 Dec 1941 At 1120 A/26, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Gibraltar.
She was escorted in by the destroyer HMS Whitehall (Lt.Cdr. A.B. Russell, RN).
The German B-Dienst was aware of P 39's passage. A signal from her giving her position as 35°46'N, 10°45'W at 2100 A/24, on an easterly course had been intercepted. (3)
2 Jan 1942 At 1430 A/2, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 2nd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Alboran Sea.
For the daily positions of HMS P 39 during this patrol see the map below.

3 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 0106 A/3, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic informed HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) of the following; HMS Alouette (Lt. R.F.R. Yarde-Buller, RNVR) leaves Gibraltar at 0700 A/3 to patrol off Melilla. (5)

3 Jan 1942 At 2302 A/3, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) received the signal timed 2206 A/3 from the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) which contained orders to intercept the German merchant vessel Elisabeth Hendrik Fisser (5014 GRT, built 1924) which was believed ready to sail from Agua Amarga, Spain. Course was set to comply with these orders. [We have been unable to find the exact text of this signal.] (4)
4 Jan 1942 At 0730 A/4, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) dived in position 36°40'N, 02°04'W and continued submerged to a position near Mesa de Roldan.

At 1200 A/4, it was seen that there were no ships either at the loading pier or at anchor in Agua Amarga. Several ships, all flting neutral colours and inside Spanish territorial water passed during daylight hours.
From 1940 A/4 to 0710 A/5, HMS P 39 patrolled on the surface between Mesa de Roldan and a line joining Cape de Gata and Cape Palos but there was no sign of the expected German ship. (4)

5 Jan 1942 Around 1000 A/5, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) inspected Agua Amarga but there was again no sign of the German ship. P 39 then patrolled off Mesa de Roldan until 1922 A/5 when she surfaced and patrolled on the surface as had been done during the previous night.

At 1245 A/5, HMS Spiraea (T/Lt. R.W. Tretheway, RNR) escorting the merchant vessel Melrose Abbey II (British, 2473 GRT, built 1936) were seen. The Melrose Abbey II was en-route from Gibraltar to Aguilas.
At 1830 A/5, HMS Spiraea was seen returning. She passed 1000 yards to the west of HMS P 39 proceeding on course 210° at 11 knots. (6)

6 Jan 1942 HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) conducted a diving patrol off Mesa de Roldan during the day. Again shipping was seen in Agua Amarga. During the night of 6/7 January HMS P 39 patrolled as in the two previous nights. (4)
7 Jan 1942 HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) conducted a diving patrol off Mesa de Roldan during the day and again no shipping at all was seen in Agua Amarga.

At 1605 A/7, the signal timed 1036 A/7 from the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) was received. This signal contained the following orders; Leave patrol after dark today and proceed to Gibraltar passing through position 36°30'N, 02°00'W. Proceed submerged by day. Crossing 05°00'W at 0830 A/9. Bombing restrictions have been arranged.
At 1920 A/7, HMS P 39 surfaced and proceeded on the surface in accordance with the signal timed 1036 A/7 from the F.O.C.N.A. (4)

8 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1836 A/8, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders / information to HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Cancel exercise with aircraft as had been ordered in my signal timed 1508 A/7. An enemy submarine is reported in the eastern entrance of the Straits. One destroyer is on patrol on longitude 05°00'W.
[No enemy submarine was however in the area on this day.] (5)
9 Jan 1942 At 1130 A/9, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Gibraltar. (4)

16 Jan 1942 At 1850 A/16, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 3rd war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Alboran Sea. She was escorted out by HMS Active (Lt.Cdr. M.W. Tomkinson, RN) until 2110 A/16. [Most likely exercises were carried out as normally submarines leaving Gibraltar eastbound were not escorted.] (4)
16 Jan 1942 For the daily positions of HMS P 39 during her 3rd war patrol see the map below. No position is known for 23 January 1942.

21 Jan 1942 At 2314 A/21, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) investigated a suspicious vessel which had been manoeuvring in the vicinity of Alboran Island for no apparent reason. This vessel was showing navigation lights and on closing was seen to be flying the Spanish Ensign. It was considered to be small patrol vessel. This vessel left the area before daylight/22. (4)

23 Jan 1942 At 2000 A/23, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Gibraltar in accordance with the orders contained in the Flag Officer Command North Atlantic's (F.O.C.N.A.) signal timed 1336 A/21. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.

In a signal timed 1037 A/23, F.O.C.N.A. had sent the following orders to HMS P 39; Do not cross 05°00'W before 1000 A/24. Arrive in position 180° - Europa Point - 1 mile at 1130 A/24. A convoy [this was OG-78] will be arriving from the west at 1000 A/24. (4)
24 Jan 1942 At 1230 A/24, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol at Gibraltar.

At 0038 A/24, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic's (F.O.C.N.A.) signal timed 2238 A/23 was received. P 39 decreased speed to comply with the orders contained in this signal. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal but most likely the convoy [OG-78] had been delayed so P 39 was ordered to arrive later as well.] (4)
2 Feb 1942 Around 1110 A/2, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for passage to Malta.
She was ordered to pass through position 38°18'N, 09°00'E at about 1600 A/8.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed. Also there is no passage report available.] (7)

9 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1217 B/9, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); HMS Cleopatra (Capt. M.S. Slattery, RN) and HMS Fortune (Lt.Cdr. R.D.H.S. Pankhurst, RN) proceeding high speed through positions 36°41'N, 11°10'E and 36°23'N, 12°28'E to position 36°12'N 13°32'E passing the second position about 0700 B/11 thence by the south-east swept channel. P 38 is to keep south of 36°15'N and west of 12°20'E until 1200 B/11. (8)
11 Feb 1942 HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) arrived at Malta from Gibraltar. Her passage had been uneventful. (7)

16 Feb 1942 At 1925 A/16, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in position 'Z' (32°46'N, 14°22'E), to the east of Tripoli to intercept an important enemy convoy together with four other submarines of the 10th submarine flotilla; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN).

The deployment off these submarines near Tripoli was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B).

Eventually, the convoys were reorganised with three more ships from Messina and sailed on 20-21 February: 1) At 1935 B/20-2105 B/20, Giulio Giordani and Lerici sailed from Taranto, escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Circe. 2) At 2205 B/20-2237 B/20, Monviso sailed from Brindisi escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini. 3) At 0345 B/21, the transport Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) sailed from Naples at 0345 B/21 escorted by the torpedo boats Orsa and Enrico Cosenz and transited the Straits of Messina at 1730 B/21. 4) At 1730 B/21-1915 B/21, the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) sailed from Messina, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale, Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Pallade and merged with the Unione convoy.

To cover the operation the following forces were deployed: 1) At 1445 B21-1514 B/21, the battleship Caio Duilio, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere sailed from Taranto. 2) At 1705 B/21-1845 B/21, the heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Antonio da Noli sailed from Messina. During the night, they caught up with the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoys.

At 0726 B/21-0754 B/21, the Giordani and Monviso convoys merged and proceeded to Corfu where they arrived at 1100 B/21-1126 B/21. At 1130 B/21-1400 B/21, they sailed from Corfu (without the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini, which later sailed for Valona).

At 0710 B/22, the Caio Duilio squadron sighted the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoy covered by the Gorizia squadron. Scirocco left the convoy as it crossed the 34th parallel and arrived at Messina at 1424 B/23. The Caio Duilio squadron turned back and arrived at Taranto at 0202 B/24-0230 B/24. The Gorizia squadron also left the convoy and returned to Messina, arriving at 1159 B/24-1340 B/24. The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23. [No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (9) 17 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1435 B/17 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Earlier ordered dispositions anticipated the convoy approaching through position 32°48'N, 14°00'E on course 270° or course 290° and possibly course - 263°. Positions may be moved by signal course - 360° or course 180° as necessary according to air reconnaissance. If forced to retire submarines have freedom of movement as follows; P 34 west of 14°05'E. Una between 14°05'E and 14°17'E. P 39 between 14°17'E and 14°28'E. P 38 and Sokol east of 14°28'E. (8) 20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1205 B/20 Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN). At 2000 B/20 proceed as follows; HMS P 34 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E to Area K off Kerkenah. HMS Una through positions 33°20'N, 13°45'E and 33°20'N, 12°55'E to Area N south of 33°45'W and west of 12°55'E. HMS P 39 was to operate in area L, 32°10'N, 15°00'W - 000° - 40 - 50. Three more submarines will sail shortly to concentrate again with HMS Una and HMS P 39 in the vicinity of Khoms. In a signal timed 2207 B/20 they were ordered by Capt. S.10 to return to their previous patrol positions by 0001 B/23. (8) 20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 2207 B/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Cancel my signals timed 1125 B/20th to P 38 and 1205 B/20th to Una, P 34 and P 39. Retire at least 50 miles within areas in my signal timed 1435 B/17th and return to the patrol positions just vacated by 0001 B/23. Further order will follow. The re-deployment / re-positioning of these submarines was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B). Eventually, the convoys were reorganised with three more ships from Messina and sailed on 20-21 February: 1) At 1935 B/20-2105 B/20, Giulio Giordani and Lerici sailed from Taranto, escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta (leader), Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Circe. 2) At 2205 B/20-2237 B/20, Monviso sailed from Brindisi escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini. 3) At 0345 B/21, the transport Unione (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1942) sailed from Naples at 0345 B/21 escorted by the torpedo boats Orsa and Enrico Cosenz and transited the Straits of Messina at 1730 B/21. 4) At 1730 B/21-1915 B/21, the transports Ravello (Italian, 6142 GRT, built 1941) and Monginevro (Italian, 5324 GRT, built 1940) sailed from Messina, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale, Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Giuseppe Dezza and Pallade and merged with the Unione convoy. To cover the operation the following forces were deployed: 1) At 1445 B21-1514 B/21, the battleship Caio Duilio, screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere sailed from Taranto. 2) At 1705 B/21-1845 B/21, the heavy cruisers Gorizia, Trento and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani and Antonio da Noli sailed from Messina. During the night, they caught up with the Ravello/Monginevro/Unione convoys. At 0726 B/21-0754 B/21, the Giordani and Monviso convoys merged and proceeded to Corfu where they arrived at 1100 B/21-1126 B/21. At 1130 B/21-1400 B/21, they sailed from Corfu (without the torpedo boat Angelo Bassini, which later sailed for Valona). At 0710 B/22, the Duilio group sighted Convoy 1 and at 0819 B/22, convoy 2. At 0920 B/22, Convoy 1 and Convoy 2 met but they did not merge. At 1039 B/22, they sighted the Duilio squadron. At 1420 B/22-1424 B/22, an enemy reconnaissance aircraft dropped a few bombs between Duilio and convoy 2. Duilio, Trento and other units opened fire to keep it at bay. At 0800 B/23, Duilio ordered Geniere to proceed to the assistance of the submarine Tricheco which was in difficulty in position 140° - 90 miles from the squadron. Due to the rough seas, Geniere was unable to do so and rejoined at 0930 B/23. At 1014 B/23, Circe detected an echo with her sonar, and the convoy turned to port to take avoiding action. At 1022 B/23, the torpedo boat dropped a pattern of depth charges set to a depth of 75 metres, the submarine was observed to break the surface, and the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare opened fire with her guns and machine guns, and later dropped depth charges, joined also by Emanuele Pessagno. An escorting aircraft also machine-gunned the spot and dropped a bomb. The two destroyers were then ordered to rejoin the convoy, and Circe continued the hunt with her sonar. The submarine broke surface again, and Emanuele Pessagno returned and dropped more depth charges, but Circe complained that she was confusing her sonar, and she sailed away. Antonio Pigafetta (convoy leader) then asked Circe how long she intended to hunt the submarine and the torpedo boat replied that a large oil slick was seen and she would investigate. She did so and found debris, one bag with three Union Jacks and two black flags, other objects and human remains. The position was 32°48'N, 14°58'30"E and the depth 350 metres. Victim of this attack was HMS P 38 which was lost with all hands. Circe rejoined the convoy at 1500 B/22. At 2000 B/22, the Duilio group turned back and reached Taranto at 0740 B/24. At 1845 B/22, the torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy. At 0750 B/23, they sighted the Third Cruiser Division, and the heavy cruiser Gorizia ordered them to join the Vivaldi convoy; this was sighted at 1050 B/23, and an hour later, the two torpedo boats took position ahead of the convoy. Convoy 1 (aka Vivaldi convoy); Unione, Ravello and Monginevro, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale and Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Pallade, Calliope and Perseo arrived in Tripoli at 1420 B/23-1730 B/23. Convoy 2 (aka Pigafetta convoy); Monviso, Lerici and Giulio Giordani, escorted by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno, Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale and the torpedo boat Circe, arrived in Tripoli at 1600 B/23-1759 B/23. The heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento (Third Cruiser Division) and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere, screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani, Antonio da Noli and Scirocco had escorted them as far as the 34°N parallel at 1000 B/23 before turning back. At 0410 B/24-0500 B/24, the torpedo boats Orsa, Generale Marcello Prestinari, Giuseppe Cesare Abba and Giuseppe Dezza sailed from Messina to reinforce the Third Cruiser Squaron and joined at 0823 B/23. They arrived in Messina at 1206 B/24-1424 B/24, except Generale Marcello Prestinari which sighted two Carley floats at 1525 B/24, 5 miles SE of Taormina, and searched for survivors. She arrived in Messina at 1730 B/24. The battleship Duilio screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere (11th Destroyer Squadron), returned to Taranto at 0202 B/24-0251 B/24. (8) 21 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1231 B/21 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); My signal timed 2207 B/20. Four submarines leave Malta through position 34°30'N, 14°10'E to positions as follows by dawn/23. P 36, P 35, Unbeaten through position 32°48'N, 14°45'E to position 'W' (32°41'N, 14°32'E), position 'T' (32°38'N, 14°42'E), position 'S' (32°44'N, 14°47'E) respectively and Upholder direct from first position given to position 'R' (32°50'N, 14°53'E). My signal timed 1435 B/17, amend and to retire from area. P 36 and P 38, between 14°28'E and 14°40'E former south latter north of 32°45'N. East of 14°40'E, Upholder north of 32°48'N, P 35 south of 32°41'N and Unbeaten between those latitudes. Convoy may be expected any time after dawn/23 and if an eastbound convoy is encountered first that proceeding westward to Tripoli is primary target and position must not be compromised before its passage. (8) 22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots. In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots. The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN). Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (8) 23 Feb 1942 While trying to close on an enemy convoy to the east of Tripoli, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) believed to have been detected and depth charged by the escorts. In fact, the depth charges were probably those of the Italian torpedo boat Circe attacking HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) which did not return from patrol and was lost with all hands. 0145 A/23 - Commenced patrolling on the surface near position 'Z' (32°46'N, 14°22'E). 0310 A/23 - Heard HE bearing 320°. HE was closing and was classified as being from a fast reciprocating engine. 0315 A/23 - Sighted an unidentified vessel bearing 325°. Dived to 90 feet. The vessel appeared to be searching, periodically stopping it's engines and dropping small charges which sounded like charges which were used during our own A/S exercises. 0430 A/23 - The HE faded away on bearing 090°. P 39 remained deep, keeping a listening watch. 0620 A/23 - Surfaced. to take a sight but found the weather to be overcast. 0635 A/23 - Dived and maintained a diving patrol. 0950 A/23 - Heard HE bearing 090°. It was closing. No surface vessel was seen. Periscope visibility was 4000 to 5000 yards. 0955 A/23 - Sighted three Cant. Z.501 aircraft flying low in 'V-formation' on the same bearing as the HE. Dived to 120 feet. HE was closing on a steady bearing of 090°. HMS P 39 retired to the northward. Between 1003 A/23 and 1045 A/23, 60 depth charges were dropped but not close. The HE of two other vessels, apparently hunting, was heard during these depth charge attacks. More distant HE was heard bearing 140° moving through 180° to 240°. 1115 A/23 - The HE was drawing away on bearing 290°. P 39 came to periscope depth. Sighted one Cant. Z.501 bearing 270° at the same time HE was reported approaching from that direction. P 39 returned to 120 feet and took avoiding action. 1145 A/23 to 1230 A/23 - The HE of two vessels, apparently hunting was heard. Also depth charges were dropped. It is doubtful that this depth charging was directed against HMS P 39 as it was all to the westward. 1300 A/23 - The HE faded out. 2100 A/23 - Set course for a position of Lampedusa in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1203 B/23. (9) 23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25. HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E. HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E. HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E. HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E. HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E. HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E. They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (8) 24 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1251 B/24, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) of the following; Area intelligence. Area G probable enemy route 35°55'N, 12°10'E to 35°05'N, 12°55'E. Small sweeper usually encountered between Lampedusa and Lampion and M.A.3. may operate from former. Areas L and N , enemy route number 7 buoy Kerkennah 33°51'N, 11°10'E - 33°06'N, 12°00'E. Coasters may anchor off Turgeuness and Zuara at night. (8) 26 Feb 1942 At 2000 A/26, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta. (9) 27 Feb 1942 At 0805 A/27, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. At 0725 A/27, while approaching the harbour and one mile off the entrance, HMS P 39 was attacked by a Me. 109 fighter. HMS P 39 dived. Some slight damage was done to the high power periscope fittings, diving compass and light cables. At 0745 A/27, HMS P 39 surfaced and entered harbour. (9) 6 Mar 1942 HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was reported to have sustained some damage during an air raid at Malta. Temporary repairs were started at Malta but full repairs were to be made in the U.K. amongst other repairs a new battery was to be installed there. (3) 25 Mar 1942 In a signal the C-in-C Mediterranean was informed that HMS P 39 could be taken in hand for repairs / refit by the Blyth Shipb. & Eng. Co., on or about 20 April. (10) 26 Mar 1942 During a heavy air raid on Malta Dockyard in the afternoon, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was heavily damaged by near misses. Her back was broken rendering the submarine a total loss.SourcesADM 199/400ADM 199/2234 ADM 199/2565 ADM 199/1833 ADM 199/2235 ADM 199/1833 + BT 389/20/240ADM 199/424ADM 199/2236 ADM 199/1224 ADM 199/2237 ADM numbers indicate documents at the British National Archives at Kew, London. Return to the Allied Warships section
The torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy and joined it at 1150 B/23. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1525 B/23-1735 B/23.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (9)

17 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1435 B/17 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Earlier ordered dispositions anticipated the convoy approaching through position 32°48'N, 14°00'E on course 270° or course 290° and possibly course - 263°. Positions may be moved by signal course - 360° or course 180° as necessary according to air reconnaissance. If forced to retire submarines have freedom of movement as follows; P 34 west of 14°05'E. Una between 14°05'E and 14°17'E. P 39 between 14°17'E and 14°28'E. P 38 and Sokol east of 14°28'E. (8)
20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1205 B/20 Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to to HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN).

At 2000 B/20 proceed as follows; HMS P 34 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E to Area K off Kerkenah. HMS Una through positions 33°20'N, 13°45'E and 33°20'N, 12°55'E to Area N south of 33°45'W and west of 12°55'E. HMS P 39 was to operate in area L, 32°10'N, 15°00'W - 000° - 40 - 50.
Three more submarines will sail shortly to concentrate again with HMS Una and HMS P 39 in the vicinity of Khoms.
In a signal timed 2207 B/20 they were ordered by Capt. S.10 to return to their previous patrol positions by 0001 B/23. (8)

20 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 2207 B/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); Cancel my signals timed 1125 B/20th to P 38 and 1205 B/20th to Una, P 34 and P 39. Retire at least 50 miles within areas in my signal timed 1435 B/17th and return to the patrol positions just vacated by 0001 B/23. Further order will follow.

The re-deployment / re-positioning of these submarines was to intercept the enemy convoy operation K 7. On 13 February, ULTRA had revealed the imminent departure of this convoy [see DEFE3/750 frames 77, 78, 153]. The transport Monviso (Italian, 5322 GRT, built 1941), escorted by the destroyer Emanuele Pessagno and the torpedo boat Lince was to leave from Brindisi. The tanker Giulio Giordani (Italian, 10534 GRT, built 1939) and the transport Lerici (Italian, 6070 GRT, built 1941) escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale, Scirocco and the torpedo boat Pallade were to leave from Taranto. They were to be screened by a task group formed around the battleship Caio Duilio. This operation was postponed, perhaps due to the presence of a Malta convoy (convoy MW 9A/B).
At 0710 B/22, the Duilio group sighted Convoy 1 and at 0819 B/22, convoy 2. At 0920 B/22, Convoy 1 and Convoy 2 met but they did not merge. At 1039 B/22, they sighted the Duilio squadron.
At 1420 B/22-1424 B/22, an enemy reconnaissance aircraft dropped a few bombs between Duilio and convoy 2. Duilio, Trento and other units opened fire to keep it at bay.

At 0800 B/23, Duilio ordered Geniere to proceed to the assistance of the submarine Tricheco which was in difficulty in position 140° - 90 miles from the squadron. Due to the rough seas, Geniere was unable to do so and rejoined at 0930 B/23.

At 1014 B/23, Circe detected an echo with her sonar, and the convoy turned to port to take avoiding action. At 1022 B/23, the torpedo boat dropped a pattern of depth charges set to a depth of 75 metres, the submarine was observed to break the surface, and the destroyer Antoniotto Usodimare opened fire with her guns and machine guns, and later dropped depth charges, joined also by Emanuele Pessagno. An escorting aircraft also machine-gunned the spot and dropped a bomb. The two destroyers were then ordered to rejoin the convoy, and Circe continued the hunt with her sonar. The submarine broke surface again, and Emanuele Pessagno returned and dropped more depth charges, but Circe complained that she was confusing her sonar, and she sailed away. Antonio Pigafetta (convoy leader) then asked Circe how long she intended to hunt the submarine and the torpedo boat replied that a large oil slick was seen and she would investigate. She did so and found debris, one bag with three Union Jacks and two black flags, other objects and human remains. The position was 32°48'N, 14°58'30"E and the depth 350 metres. Victim of this attack was HMS P 38 which was lost with all hands. Circe rejoined the convoy at 1500 B/22.
At 2000 B/22, the Duilio group turned back and reached Taranto at 0740 B/24.

At 1845 B/22, the torpedo boats Calliope and Perseo sailed from Tripoli to reinforce the convoy. At 0750 B/23, they sighted the Third Cruiser Division, and the heavy cruiser Gorizia ordered them to join the Vivaldi convoy; this was sighted at 1050 B/23, and an hour later, the two torpedo boats took position ahead of the convoy.

Convoy 1 (aka Vivaldi convoy); Unione, Ravello and Monginevro, escorted by the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Lanzerotto Malocello, Premuda, Strale and Nicolò Zeno and the torpedo boats Pallade, Calliope and Perseo arrived in Tripoli at 1420 B/23-1730 B/23.

Convoy 2 (aka Pigafetta convoy); Monviso, Lerici and Giulio Giordani, escorted by the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno, Antonio Pigafetta, Antoniotto Usodimare, Maestrale and the torpedo boat Circe, arrived in Tripoli at 1600 B/23-1759 B/23.

The heavy cruisers Gorizia and Trento (Third Cruiser Division) and the light cruiser Giovanni Delle Bande Nere, screened by the destroyers Alpino, Alfredo Oriani, Antonio da Noli and Scirocco had escorted them as far as the 34°N parallel at 1000 B/23 before turning back. At 0410 B/24-0500 B/24, the torpedo boats Orsa, Generale Marcello Prestinari, Giuseppe Cesare Abba and Giuseppe Dezza sailed from Messina to reinforce the Third Cruiser Squaron and joined at 0823 B/23. They arrived in Messina at 1206 B/24-1424 B/24, except Generale Marcello Prestinari which sighted two Carley floats at 1525 B/24, 5 miles SE of Taormina, and searched for survivors. She arrived in Messina at 1730 B/24.
The battleship Duilio screened by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Ascari and Geniere (11th Destroyer Squadron), returned to Taranto at 0202 B/24-0251 B/24. (8)

21 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1231 B/21 the Capt. S.10 sent the following information / orders to HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN); My signal timed 2207 B/20. Four submarines leave Malta through position 34°30'N, 14°10'E to positions as follows by dawn/23. P 36, P 35, Unbeaten through position 32°48'N, 14°45'E to position 'W' (32°41'N, 14°32'E), position 'T' (32°38'N, 14°42'E), position 'S' (32°44'N, 14°47'E) respectively and Upholder direct from first position given to position 'R' (32°50'N, 14°53'E). My signal timed 1435 B/17, amend and to retire from area. P 36 and P 38, between 14°28'E and 14°40'E former south latter north of 32°45'N. East of 14°40'E, Upholder north of 32°48'N, P 35 south of 32°41'N and Unbeaten between those latitudes. Convoy may be expected any time after dawn/23 and if an eastbound convoy is encountered first that proceeding westward to Tripoli is primary target and position must not be compromised before its passage. (8)

22 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1018 B/22, the Capt. S.10 informed all submarines of the 10th Submarine Flotilla which were at sea that at 0925 B/22 three merchant ships, two cruisers and ten destroyers had been sighted in position 35°18'N, 18°40'E proceeding on course 190° at speeds of 8 to 12 knots

In a signal timed 1111 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that eight merchant merchant ships, two battleships, five cruisers and fifteen destroyers had been sighted in position 35°12'N, 18°22'E on course 190° at a speed of 8 knots.

In a signal timed 1336 B/22, the Capt. S.10 reported that an enemy battleship of the Littorio-class, two cruisers of the Trento / Condottieri-class, four destroyers, three merchant ships and three enemy aircraft had been reported in position 34°48'N, 18°30'E proceeding on course 190° at a speed of 10 knots.

The following submarines were the ones that were on patrol at that time and thus must have received these signals; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN), HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN), HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN).

Presumably HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, DSO and Bar, RN) also received these signals as she was also on patrol but she was to the north of Sicily at that time so given the position of the convoy sighted the signals were of no use to her. (8)

23 Feb 1942 While trying to close on an enemy convoy to the east of Tripoli, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) believed to have been detected and depth charged by the escorts. In fact, the depth charges were probably those of the Italian torpedo boat Circe attacking HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) which did not return from patrol and was lost with all hands.
0145 A/23 - Commenced patrolling on the surface near position 'Z' (32°46'N, 14°22'E).
0310 A/23 - Heard HE bearing 320°. HE was closing and was classified as being from a fast reciprocating engine.

0315 A/23 - Sighted an unidentified vessel bearing 325°. Dived to 90 feet. The vessel appeared to be searching, periodically stopping it's engines and dropping small charges which sounded like charges which were used during our own A/S exercises.
0430 A/23 - The HE faded away on bearing 090°. P 39 remained deep, keeping a listening watch.
0620 A/23 - Surfaced. to take a sight but found the weather to be overcast.
0635 A/23 - Dived and maintained a diving patrol.
0950 A/23 - Heard HE bearing 090°. It was closing. No surface vessel was seen. Periscope visibility was 4000 to 5000 yards.

0955 A/23 - Sighted three Cant. Z.501 aircraft flying low in 'V-formation' on the same bearing as the HE. Dived to 120 feet. HE was closing on a steady bearing of 090°. HMS P 39 retired to the northward.

Between 1003 A/23 and 1045 A/23, 60 depth charges were dropped but not close. The HE of two other vessels, apparently hunting, was heard during these depth charge attacks. More distant HE was heard bearing 140° moving through 180° to 240°.

1115 A/23 - The HE was drawing away on bearing 290°. P 39 came to periscope depth. Sighted one Cant. Z.501 bearing 270° at the same time HE was reported approaching from that direction. P 39 returned to 120 feet and took avoiding action.

1145 A/23 to 1230 A/23 - The HE of two vessels, apparently hunting was heard. Also depth charges were dropped. It is doubtful that this depth charging was directed against HMS P 39 as it was all to the westward.
1300 A/23 - The HE faded out.
2100 A/23 - Set course for a position of Lampedusa in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1203 B/23. (9)

23 Feb 1942 In signals timed 1152 B/23 and 1203 B/23, Capt. S.10 sent the following orders; HMS Una (Lt. C.P. Norman, RN) and HMS P 38 (Lt. R.J. Hemingway, DSC, RN) leave their patrol positions at 2000 B/23 through positions 35°00'N, 14°12'E, and 35°00'N, 14°18'E respectively to arrive off Marsamxett harbour at 0715 B/25.
HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'K', 34°20'N, 11°10'E - 000° - 40 - 60 through position 33°25'N, 13°45'E.
HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'G', 35°05'N, 12°05'E - 000° - 50 - 40 through positions 33°05'N, 14°11'E and 35°00'N, 13°05'E.

HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'L', south of 34°15'N and west of 11°40'E. She was to proceed through positions 33°00'N, 14°11'E and 33°50'N, 13°20'E.

HMS Unbeaten (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'H', 35°15'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 50 proceeding through positions 33°00'N, 14°32'E and 35°00'N, 12°30'E.

HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2100 B/23 to proceed to area 'N', south of 33°45'N and between 11° 50'E and 12°55'E proceeding through positions 32°50'N, 14°45'E and 33.20'N, 13.45'E.

HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) was to leave her patrol position at 2000 B/23 to proceed to area 'J', 36°54'N, 15°24'E - 330° - 40 - 70 proceeding through positions 32°38'N, 15°00'E and 35°00'N, 15°30'E.
They were not to attack submarines encountered during the night of 23/24 February to prevent friendly fire incidents from happening. (8)

24 Feb 1942 In a signal timed 1251 B/24, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, VC, DSO, RN), HMS P 36 (Lt. H.N. Edmonds, DSC, RN) and HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) of the following; Area intelligence. Area G probable enemy route 35°55'N, 12°10'E to 35°05'N, 12°55'E. Small sweeper usually encountered between Lampedusa and Lampion and M.A.3. may operate from former. Areas L and N , enemy route number 7 buoy Kerkennah 33°51'N, 11°10'E - 33°06'N, 12°00'E. Coasters may anchor off Turgeuness and Zuara at night. (8)
26 Feb 1942 At 2000 A/26, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta. (9)
27 Feb 1942 At 0805 A/27, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta.

At 0725 A/27, while approaching the harbour and one mile off the entrance, HMS P 39 was attacked by a Me. 109 fighter. HMS P 39 dived. Some slight damage was done to the high power periscope fittings, diving compass and light cables. At 0745 A/27, HMS P 39 surfaced and entered harbour. (9)
6 Mar 1942 HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was reported to have sustained some damage during an air raid at Malta.
Temporary repairs were started at Malta but full repairs were to be made in the U.K. amongst other repairs a new battery was to be installed there. (3)
25 Mar 1942 In a signal the C-in-C Mediterranean was informed that HMS P 39 could be taken in hand for repairs / refit by the Blyth Shipb. & Eng. Co., on or about 20 April. (10)

26 Mar 1942 During a heavy air raid on Malta Dockyard in the afternoon, HMS P 39 (Lt. N. Marriott, DSC, RN) was heavily damaged by near misses. Her back was broken rendering the submarine a total loss.
ADM numbers indicate documents at the British National Archives at Kew, London.
